page: Amazon Web Services r5b.4xlarge is a memory-optimized instance built on the Nitro hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an Intel Xeon 8259CL processor operating at 3.1 GHz. The configuration includes 16 virtual CPUs across 8 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled, supported by 128.0 GB of DDR4 memory running at 3 GHz. This configuration yields a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB of RAM per core. No local storage or GPU hardware is bundled with this instance, and it provides a baseline network bandwidth of 5 Gbps.

Benchmark results place the processing capabilities of this instance in the average to weak tiers. Single-core performance is weak, as demonstrated by stress-ng single-core CPU and single-threaded gzip compression tests. However, multi-core operations, including stress-ng multi-core and bzip3 multi-threaded decompression, achieve average tier results. Memory bandwidth tests for both small cached blocks and large uncached blocks consistently register in the average category, reflecting stable memory throughput.

Given the 8:1 memory-to-vCPU ratio and average database operation benchmarks, this instance is configured for memory-intensive workloads that do not require high-speed local storage. It is suitable for in-memory databases like Redis, where it achieves average performance, as well as medium-scale data processing and memory-bound enterprise applications. The primary tradeoff is the lower single-core CPU performance, making it less suitable for compute-heavy or single-threaded tasks.
description: Amazon Web Services r5b.4xlarge is an x86_64 memory-optimized instance featuring 16 vCPUs and 128.0 GB of DDR4 RAM, running on the Nitro hypervisor. Powered by an Intel Xeon 8259CL processor at 3.1 GHz, it delivers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. The instance does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, and it operates with a baseline network bandwidth of 5 Gbps. Benchmark data indicates average multi-core CPU and memory bandwidth performance, contrasted by weak single-core processing speeds. This hardware profile is suited for memory-intensive applications, in-memory caching, and database operations that do not require local NVMe storage, offering a balanced resource density for memory-bound workloads.
